does: This paper identifies and exploits safety vulnerabilities in multimodal large language models by introducing PolyJailbreak, a black-box jailbreaking framework that targets text-vision scenarios. The authors discover multimodal safety asymmetry, where visual alignment creates uneven safety constraints across modalities, weakening overall robustness. Through analysis of attention dynamics and latent representations, they show that visual inputs can disrupt cross-modal information flow and impair the model's ability to distinguish benign from malicious intents. PolyJailbreak operationalizes these vulnerabilities by organizing them into a library of Atomic Strategy Primitives that enable step-wise input transformations to bypass safety mechanisms and elicit harmful responses from state-of-the-art MLLMs without requiring model access or gradient information.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of jailbreak attacks against large language models, extending prior text-only adversarial research to the multimodal setting. It connects to foundational safety alignment work in LLMs while responding to the emerging question of how safety mechanisms transfer when models process multiple input modalities. The paper engages with literature on cross-modal reasoning in vision-language models and draws on recent findings about modality-specific vulnerabilities. It also relates to work analyzing attention mechanisms and information flow in neural networks, applying these interpretability techniques to understand why visual inputs create new attack surfaces that text-only defenses fail to address.
enables: This research opens directions for developing more robust multimodal safety mechanisms that account for cross-modal vulnerabilities rather than treating modalities independently. It motivates future work on detecting and defending against coordinated jailbreak attacks that exploit asymmetries between visual and textual alignment. The structured library of attack primitives could guide the development of evaluation benchmarks for assessing multimodal robustness. The findings also suggest the need for unified safety training approaches that jointly optimize constraints across modalities rather than treating them separately, potentially informing more resilient alignment techniques for emerging multimodal systems.
